Notably, Islam diverges from Catholicism and Christian Orthodoxy in its absence of a formal priesthood. Instead, Islamic scholars or religious sheikhs, esteemed figures within Islamic communities, fulfill the role of imam, or “prayer leader.” The imam’s responsibilities encompass leading congregational prayers, officiating marriages and funerals, and providing spiritual guidance and interpretation of Islamic teachings. Seeking counsel from a local imam can be beneficial for Arab American Muslims grappling with healthcare decisions (Mpofu 2018).
sidebar.para: The degree of religious observance varies among individuals, ranging from nominal adherence to devout practice. However, owing to the intertwined nature of religion and governance in many Arab countries, a certain level of religious engagement is culturally expected. For instance, Islamic principles profoundly influence various facets of life in Jordan, among other Arab countries. Under Islamic law, abortion is considered a criminal act, while foster parenting is encouraged, although adoption is prohibited (Büchler and Schneider Kayasseh 2018). Infertility treatments sanctioned by Islamic jurists are the only options available. Family matters, including marriage, divorce, guardianship, and inheritance, are adjudicated in Islamic law courts, applying shariah.

Public schools incorporate Islamic education and provide prayer spaces and schedules are adjusted to accommodate Islamic holidays and the weekly congregational prayer on Fridays. During Ramadan, businesses close during daylight hours, and workdays are abbreviated to facilitate fasting. Friday marks the beginning of the weekend to enable attendance at communal prayers. Also, adhering to Islamic tradition, which mandates respect and protection for followers of other monotheistic faiths, Christians have separate religious institutions and educational facilities. In Saudi Arabia, however, the practice of religions other than Islam is officially prohibited. For Arab American Christians, church involvement holds significant cultural and spiritual importance. Many partake in fasting and participate in ceremonial church services to observe Catholic and Orthodox Christian holidays. Christian symbols, such as crosses and depictions of the Virgin Mary, may be displayed or worn. Also, educational institutions often provide classes on Christianity to reinforce religious teachings and traditions.

sidebar.title: Meaning of Life and Individual Sources of StrengthFor Muslims, Islam embodies the concept of “submission to Allah,” representing a profound commitment to the divine will and guidance. Life’s purpose revolves around devout worship and spiritual devotion, aimed at preparing for the afterlife by fulfilling religious obligations outlined in the Qur’an, Islam’s holy scripture, and the hadith, the recorded sayings, and actions of the Prophet Muhammad. Central to Islamic practice are the five pillars, or fundamental duties, which include the declaration of faith, performing the five daily prayers, giving alms to the needy, observing fasting during the holy month of Ramadan, and undertaking the pilgrimage to Mecca, known as Hajj.
sidebar.para: Despite the strong emphasis on familial ties and interconnectedness in Arab societies, religious faith often holds the utmost significance. Whether adherents of Islam or Christianity, Arabs typically exhibit a profound identification with their religious communities, with religious affiliation constituting a fundamental aspect of personal identity alongside family lineage. In everyday life, the presence and influence of God and His divine power are acknowledged and revered, shaping individuals’ beliefs, actions, and interactions within their communities and broader society.

sidebar.title: Spiritual Beliefs and Health Care Practices Many Muslims integrate spiritual practices into their approach to healthcare, often combining spiritual remedies such as prayer and Quranic recitation with conventional medical treatment. Devout patients may request specific accommodations, such as facing Mecca during prayers and ensuring cleanliness through ritual ablution before worship. In Islamic tradition, cleanliness is predominant, as prayers are only deemed acceptable when performed in a clean environment, with a clean body and attire. Hygiene is also emphasized, reflecting a belief in the importance of cleanliness for both physical and spiritual health. Also, Muslims are encouraged to support their communities by visiting the sick and assisting those in need, reflecting a sense of social responsibility and compassion. Illness is sometimes perceived as a form of divine punishment for sins, though many Muslims view it as a test or trial that serves to expiate sins and strengthen character. Responses to illness often include patience, endurance, and unwavering hope in Allah’s mercy with devout individuals believing that even seemingly irreversible conditions may be cured if they align with Allah’s will. Suffering is seen as a means of earning greater rewards in the afterlife, reinforcing the belief in the sanctity of life and prohibiting practices like euthanasia and assisted suicide. Arab American Christians share similar spiritual beliefs regarding healthcare with Orthodox or Catholic Christians. They prioritize caring for the body and observe similar burial practices. A visit from a priest is customary, especially for Catholic patients who may receive the sacrament of the sick as part of their spiritual care. This intertwining of faith and healthcare underscores the importance of spirituality in providing comfort and support to individuals facing illness or adversity within Arab American communities.

Health-Seeking Beliefs and Behaviors Health-seeking beliefs and behaviors among individuals of Arab heritage are deeply intertwined with cultural, religious, and social factors, shaping their approach to wellness and healthcare utilization (Aseeri 2020; Zimmerman and Shaw Jr 2020). Arab culture emphasizes the significance of familial and community ties, influencing health-seeking behaviors. Seeking advice and support from family members, friends, and community leaders is common practice. Decision-making regarding healthcare often involves consulting with elders or respected community members reflecting the collective nature of decision-making within Arab families. Religious beliefs, particularly within Islam, profoundly impact health-seeking behaviors. Islamic teachings emphasize the importance of maintaining both physical and spiritual well-being. Prayer, Quranic recitation, and adherence to religious rituals are integrated into healthcare practices. Many Muslims believe in the healing power of faith and may combine spiritual practices with conventional medical treatment, viewing illness as a test of faith and an opportunity for spiritual growth.
sidebar.para: Traditional healing practices, such as herbal remedies and cupping therapy, are prevalent among individuals of Arab heritage. These practices, rooted in cultural traditions and passed down through generations, are often utilized alongside modern healthcare. While some traditional remedies complement conventional treatment, others may be preferred due to cultural preferences or beliefs, highlighting the importance of holistic approaches to health. Access to healthcare services significantly influences health-seeking behaviors. In regions with limited healthcare infrastructure, individuals may rely more on home remedies or traditional healers. Conversely, in areas with advanced healthcare systems, access to hospitals, clinics, and trained medical professionals shapes health-seeking behaviors, underscoring the role of healthcare accessibility in influencing health outcomes.

The stigma surrounding certain health conditions, particularly mental health disorders, may deter individuals from seeking timely medical attention within Arab communities (Abo-Rass et al. 2024; Sweileh 2024). Cultural taboos or misconceptions about illness can contribute to delays in seeking care or reluctance to discuss health concerns openly highlighting the need for culturally sensitive healthcare practices. Gender roles and expectations impact health-seeking behaviors, with women often assuming the role of primary caregivers. Cultural norms regarding modesty and gender segregation may influence access to certain healthcare services, particularly for women’s health issues. Modesty concerns may lead to reluctance in seeking care, particularly if it involves disrobing or examination by male physicians.

Among Arab immigrants or diaspora communities, health-seeking behaviors may evolve due to exposure to different healthcare systems (Abo-Rass et al. 2024; Aseeri 2020). Acculturation may lead to a blend of traditional practices with mainstream healthcare approaches as individuals navigate cultural identity and integration into new societies, highlighting the dynamic nature of health beliefs and practices. Socioeconomic factors, including education level and income influence health-seeking behaviors. Higher education and socioeconomic status correlate with better access to healthcare services and increased health literacy leading to proactive health-seeking behaviors and preventive care practices. However, disparities in access to care may persist among marginalized populations, underscoring the importance of addressing socioeconomic barriers to healthcare access.

While Arab Americans may readily seek care for acute symptoms, preventive care utilization such as well-child visits and health screenings may be limited, particularly among low-income populations. Cultural factors, including present orientation and reluctance to plan, contribute to the underutilization of preventive services. Arab Americans prioritize coping with existing stressors and illnesses over preventive measures, highlighting the need for culturally tailored approaches to preventive care delivery. Understanding these multifaceted influences is crucial for healthcare providers to deliver culturally competent care that respects the beliefs and preferences of individuals from Arab heritage. By addressing cultural, religious, and social factors, healthcare providers can foster trust, enhance communication, and promote equitable access to healthcare services within Arab communities.

sidebar.title: Responsibility for Healthcare Responsibility for healthcare among individuals of Arab heritage is deeply rooted in cultural, familial, and religious beliefs. In Arab communities, the family plays a central role with members assuming caregiving roles for ill relatives and seeking guidance from elders in healthcare decision-making. Religious teachings, particularly within Islam, emphasize the importance of physical and spiritual well-being, integrating prayer and religious rituals into healthcare practices. While familial and religious factors shape healthcare decisions, individuals also bear accountability for their health. Arab communities prioritize preventive measures and encourage individuals to seek medical care when needed. Community support further enhances healthcare access with organizations and religious institutions offering assistance and advocacy.
sidebar.para: Healthcare providers are respected authority figures in Arab culture, with patients trusting their expertise and guidance. However, differences in healthcare approaches between Arab and American cultures can lead to misunderstandings. American healthcare emphasizes individualism and informed decision-making, while Arab culture values familism and submission to God’s will in healthcare matters. Regarding medical disclosure, Arab physicians may prioritize withholding grave diagnoses to preserve patients’ hope contrasting with American practices. Healthcare financing also differs with communal resources and government facilities often serving as primary means of healthcare access in Arab countries. Understanding and respecting these cultural nuances are crucial for healthcare providers to deliver effective and culturally sensitive care to individuals of Arab heritage. By acknowledging the multifaceted nature of responsibility for healthcare and bridging cultural gaps, providers can ensure better healthcare outcomes for Arab patients.

sidebar.title: Folk Practices Folk practices among people of Arab heritage offer a window into the cultural tapestry that shapes daily life and community interactions (El-Shamy 2018). These practices, deeply rooted in tradition, religion, and social customs reflect a blend of superstitions, healing traditions, and rituals that have been passed down through generations. Despite Islamic teachings discouraging superstition, beliefs in the evil eye, envy, and supernatural entities like jinn persist in Arab folklore (El-Shamy 2018). Protective measures such as wearing amulets, reciting Quranic verses and invoking the name of Allah are common practices to ward off the evil eye. Concerns about jealousy and envy are particularly associated with the misfortune caused by the evil gaze.
sidebar.para: Traditional healing practices based on the Prophet’s teachings and the theory of humor are prevalent in Arab culture. Remedies often involve treating illnesses with their opposites, such as using hot treatments for cold ailments. Cupping therapy, cautery, and herbal remedies are favored methods alongside spiritual interventions like prayers and dietary adjustments. Rituals seeking divine protection and blessings play a significant role in Arab folk practices. These rituals may involve reciting prayers from the Quran, performing blessings for newborns or homes, or seeking intercession from saints or revered figures for specific needs or challenges. Arab communities mark significant life events with celebratory traditions, including weddings, births, and religious holidays. These occasions are characterized by communal gatherings, music, dance, and feasting, fostering social cohesion and cultural expression.

Cultural taboos and customs shape behavior within Arab communities, guiding interactions and social norms. Practices such as avoiding disrespectful gestures and upholding hospitality and respect for elders are deeply ingrained cultural values. While not universally practiced, some Arab communities engage in astrological or divinatory practices for guidance or insight into the future. Astrology, palmistry, and dream interpretation hold cultural significance for those who adhere to these beliefs. Music and dance are integral components of Arab folk culture, serving as avenues for artistic expression, storytelling, and community bonding. Traditional instruments like the oud and tabla accompany regional dance styles, fostering a sense of cultural identity and heritage. Solemn ceremonies and collective expressions of grief characterize Arab mourning rituals. Customs such as washing and shrouding the deceased, reciting prayers, and observing mourning periods reflect the cultural significance placed on honoring the departed. These folk practices underscore the richness and complexity of Arab culture, evolving while preserving cultural identity and heritage. Despite variations across regions and communities, these traditions continue to shape the lives and experiences of individuals of Arab heritage bridging the past with the present in a tapestry of shared beliefs and customs.

sidebar.title: Barriers to Healthcare Newly arrived and unskilled refugees from poorer parts of the Arab world face significant challenges in accessing healthcare services in their host countries. Factors such as refugee status, recent arrival, cultural differences, language barriers, financial constraints, and lack of awareness about available services compound the stresses of immigration and impact both health status and responses to health problems (Childress et al. 2024; Mohsin et al. 2024). These individuals are at particular risk for increased exposure to ill health and inadequate access to care due to a combination of cultural, linguistic, socioeconomic, and systemic barriers.
sidebar.para: Language and communication barriers pose significant challenges for recent Arab immigrants, as limited proficiency in the host country’s language impedes effective communication with healthcare providers. Without clear communication channels, misunderstandings may arise, leading to suboptimal care and reluctance to seek medical attention. Also, cultural competence among healthcare providers is essential to address the diverse needs and beliefs of Arab patients. Cultural misunderstandings, stereotypes, and biases may further hinder access to quality care and erode trust in healthcare professionals.

The stigma surrounding mental health issues and seeking psychological support is prevalent in many Arab communities contributing to underutilization of mental healthcare services and untreated mental health conditions. Socioeconomic disparities, including poverty and lack of health insurance coverage, further exacerbate barriers to healthcare access limiting preventive care and specialized treatments for vulnerable individuals. Immigration status and documentation concerns also deter undocumented immigrants or those with uncertain status from seeking medical care, fearing legal repercussions or deportation.

Cultural mistrust and discrimination, compounded by historical biases and negative portrayals of Arabs in media and society create additional barriers to healthcare access (Alsaidi et al. 2023). Arab individuals may fear discrimination or bias from healthcare providers based on ethnicity, religion, or national origin, leading to avoidance of healthcare services or selective disclosure of information. Limited availability of culturally competent care providers proficient in the Arabic language and familiar with Arab customs further delays access to quality care, particularly in regions or healthcare settings where such providers are scarce.

Community and family dynamics, including patriarchal structures and gender roles, also influence healthcare decision-making and access among individuals of Arab heritage. Cultural norms regarding modesty, privacy, and familial obligations may impact individuals’ autonomy in seeking healthcare, with women and younger family members often deferring to elder relatives or male guardians. Addressing these complex barriers to healthcare requires a comprehensive approach that prioritizes cultural competence, linguistic accessibility, and equitable access to resources and services. Healthcare systems and providers must actively work to foster inclusive environments, promote diversity in the workforce, and implement policies and practices that recognize and address the unique needs of individuals with Arab heritage. By addressing these barriers, healthcare systems can enhance access to care, improve health outcomes, and promote health equity for individuals of Arab heritage.

sidebar.title: Cultural Responses to Health and Illness Arabs regard pain as unpleasant and something to be controlled, often expecting immediate relief postoperatively due to their confidence in medical science. However, this expectation, combined with conserving energy for recovery, may lead to reluctance in adhering to postoperative routines like frequent ambulation. While expressive responses to pain are typically reserved for immediate family, during childbirth or for illnesses accompanied by spasms, Arabs may express pain more openly (Abudoush et al. 2023; Maani et al. 2021; Maki et al. 2022). This differing response between family and healthcare providers can sometimes lead to conflicting perceptions of pain relief adequacy.
sidebar.para: The stigma surrounding mental illness is pervasive within Arab communities, often leading to denial, attributions to “bad nerves” or blame on evil spirits. Lebanese families for example, may deny mental illness altogether fearing community reactions, resulting in underreporting and avoidance of professional help (Pampati et al. 2018). Consequently, mental distress may manifest as vague complaints like abdominal pain or lassitude, and patients may prefer somatic treatments over counseling with prayer and herbal remedies being more acceptable than psychiatric intervention. Hospitalization may also be resisted due to fears of abandonment further complicating access to mental healthcare. Similarly, attitudes toward disabilities are generally negative with low expectations for education and rehabilitation. Misconceptions about mental retardation persist due to limited exposure and social stigma resulting in the disabled being kept from public view. Family members typically assume caregiving responsibilities, often indulging and protecting the disabled person reflecting acceptance of dependency within Arab culture. These cultural responses underscore the importance of understanding and respecting cultural nuances in healthcare delivery. By acknowledging and addressing these beliefs and attitudes, healthcare providers can foster trust, improve patient-provider communication, and ultimately enhance healthcare outcomes within Arab communities.

sidebar.title: Blood Transfusions and Organ Donation Blood transfusions and organ donation are significant medical procedures within Arab communities, each carrying cultural and religious implications. Blood transfusions are generally accepted among Arabs and Arab Americans, especially in emergencies or for treating severe conditions like anemia or bleeding (Almutairi et al. 2018; Braester et al. 2022). While saving lives often overrides religious concerns in emergencies, conservative Muslim communities may have religious beliefs that limit the acceptance of blood transfusions (Almutairi et al. 2018; Braester et al. 2020). Healthcare providers must ensure individuals understand the necessity and implications of blood transfusions, respecting their autonomy and providing adequate information for informed consent.
sidebar.para: Organ donation remains contentious among Arabs and Arab Americans due to religious beliefs, cultural values, and ethical considerations. Views on organ donation vary, influenced by beliefs about death, the afterlife, and body sanctity (Abdulrazeq et al. 2020; Ali et al. 2020; Al-Qerem et al. 2022; Doerry et al. 2022; Janahi et al. 2018; Mekkodathil et al. 2020). Some religious scholars permit it under specific conditions while others oppose it due to concerns about desecration or divine will. Cultural norms, familial expectations, and societal taboos also influence attitudes toward organ donation. Low donation rates are attributed to a lack of public education, misconceptions, and limited support from religious authorities. Providers must approach organ donation discussions sensitively, considering diverse perspectives and engaging in respectful dialog.

In summary, while blood transfusions are generally accepted, organ donation remains complex due to arying religious, cultural, and ethical considerations. Sensitivity and understanding of individual beliefs are crucial for healthcare providers to support informed decision-making and promote acceptance of organ donation within Arab communities.

sidebar.title: Traditional Versus Biomedical Providers Arab Americans exhibit a unique blend of traditional and biomedical healthcare practices, often seeking treatment in the United States, especially for complex health conditions. They possess a deep respect for Western medicine, valuing treatments that involve surgery, removal of causative agents, or intravenous therapies aimed at eradicating ailments. However, while they hold high regard for physical medical interventions, there is often a lack of trust or respect for mental health treatments among many Arab Americans. A prevailing sentiment among Arab Americans is that mental health services are not effective and are only necessary for individuals with severe mental disorders. Consequently, mental health services are underutilized within Arab American communities despite a greater need for such services among distressed immigrant populations. This skepticism toward mental health treatment may stem from cultural beliefs and stigmas surrounding mental illness.
sidebar.para: Gender and age considerations also play significant roles in healthcare interactions among Arab Americans. In Arab societies, interactions between unrelated males and females are uncommon with healthcare settings often segregated by sex. Shyness in women is valued and traditional Arab customs may lead Muslim men to ignore women out of politeness. Consequently, some Arab Americans may feel embarrassed or stressed when interacting with healthcare providers of the opposite sex. This discomfort may manifest as a reluctance to discuss personal information or undergo physical assessments and hygiene procedures. Arab American women may prefer to be seen by male healthcare providers, potentially excluding or denying men the opportunity to interact with or diagnose health conditions accurately, particularly for high-risk female patients within the Arab American community.

sidebar.title: Status of Healthcare Providers Arab Americans hold science and medicine in high regard, often recognizing and celebrating the historical contributions of Arabs to the field of medicine. They view doctors as authoritative figures with significant knowledge and power. When seeking medical care, Arab American patients who lack English proficiency typically prefer to see Arabic-speaking doctors due to a sense of cultural and linguistic affinity. Many believe that Arab American doctors understand their cultural background and communication style better, making them feel more comfortable and at ease during medical consultations. However, among patients who are fluent in English, there is typically no preference for Arab doctors over American doctors. Some English-speaking patients even prefer American doctors, perceiving them as more professional and respectful than their Arab American counterparts.
sidebar.para: In Arab society, medicine is highly esteemed, while nursing is often seen as a less desirable profession due to societal norms that dictate certain behaviors for women. However, efforts are being made in many Arab countries to improve the image of nursing and enhance the working environment for nurses (Wehbe-Alamah et al. 2024). American nurses are generally viewed more favorably due to their education, expertise, and ability to perform tasks traditionally reserved for physicians such as physical examinations. Younger Arab American immigrants, particularly those from Lebanon, Iraq, and Jordan tend to have more positive perceptions of nursing as a profession compared to the older generation. The hierarchical structure of Arab society is reflected in the roles and status of physicians and nurses. Physicians, typically older males, are seen as authority figures who expect nurses to defer to them in decision-making and information disclosure. Nurses, often younger females, conform to these expectations and may be relegated to roles as medical assistants and housekeepers rather than being recognized as critical thinkers and health educators. However, nursing is gradually gaining recognition as a profession in many Arab countries, with the establishment of professional organizations resembling the American Nurses Association.

sect2.title: 8.3.1 Reflective Exercise 8.2
sect2.para: Suha Abul Mona, an unmarried 23-year-old Arab woman, visits the clinic accompanied by her Middle Eastern parents and older brother. Suha complains of left lower quadrant abdominal pain and spotting, causing concern for her family. Despite feeling visibly uncomfortable and anxious, Suha’s family expresses worry about her condition.

Describing a persistent, dull pain in her left lower abdomen, Suha rates it as a 6 out of 10 on the pain scale, noting it began a day ago and has since worsened. She mentions experiencing light vaginal spotting for the past 2 days, which is unusual for her menstrual cycle. Suha denies any history of irregular periods or recent changes in menstrual pattern, attributing her intermittent nausea to the abdominal discomfort. Having no significant medical history, surgeries, or chronic conditions, Suha denies recent illnesses, travel, or exposure to infectious diseases. She is not currently taking any medications, including contraceptives.

The family actively participates in Suha’s healthcare decisions, expressing deep concern for her well-being. The nurse observes how traditional gender roles and familial expectations influence communication dynamics with Suha’s mother taking the lead in expressing health concerns while her father and older brother provide support. The nurse acknowledges the influence of Suha’s unmarried status on discussions about reproductive health and sexual activity within the family, respecting cultural norms and societal expectations.

Performing a thorough physical examination, the nurse evaluates Suha’s abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding ordering laboratory tests like a complete blood count and urinalysis to assess for underlying causes. Maintaining confidentiality regarding sensitive health information, particularly related to sexual health and reproductive issues, is essential for Suha’s privacy and comfort.

listitem.para: What steps should the nurse take in this situation?
How can the nurse employ culturally sensitive approaches to effectively address the needs of the Abul Muna family?
How can the nurse ensure the best care for Suha?
Is it appropriate for the nurse to discuss the possibility of pregnancy with Suha when her parents are not present?

sect2.title: 8.3.2 Reflective Exercise 8.2.1
sect2.para: Latif, a 75-year-old man diagnosed with end-stage liver cancer, is undergoing chemotherapy treatment. Experiencing constant nausea and decreased appetite as side effects of his cytotoxic medications, Latif often refuses to eat the food provided to him.

Recognizing Latif’s eating difficulties, the nurse arranges for the dietician to assess his dietary preferences and provide personalized recommendations. During the visit, Latif expresses concern about the halal status of the meat in his meals, indicating his discomfort with consuming non-halal food. Despite the nurse’s explanation that the food was selected based on his dietary agreement, Latif remains uneasy about the meat’s halal status. Also, he voices dissatisfaction with the lack of vegetables and salads in his meals, believing them to be essential for nutrition.

listitem.para: Drawing from insights into Arab cultural norms, what steps could the nurse take in response to Latif’s concerns?
How can the nurse and the dietician collaborate to improve Latif’s dietary intake in a culturally sensitive manner?
What strategies can the nurse employ to prevent similar situations from occurring in the future?

sect2.title: 8.3.3 Reflective Exercise 8.2.3
sect2.para: Ms. Al-Mansoori is a 32-year-old pregnant woman who arrives at the delivery suite experiencing contractions. As the nurses assist Ms. Al-Mansoori to her bed, her husband contacts the nurse and expresses his preference for no men to enter his wife’s room. He insists that all nurses, doctors, and other staff attending to his wife be female. Also, he requests that no male personnel enter the room during the night as his wife will have her veil removed while she sleeps. The nurse faces a dilemma as the doctor on duty in the delivery suite is male and Ms. Al-Mansoori is fully dilated and prepared to deliver imminently. In response, the nurse places a note outside the Al-Mansoori family’s room, indicating that males are not permitted inside based on the patient’s preference.

listitem.para: Assess the nurse’s reaction based on your nursing education.
Interpret the cultural significance behind Mr. Al-Mansoori’s actions.
What actions can the nurse take in this scenario?
